metadata.summary: Catalog of 40+ MCP servers organized by service category. Configure servers for Claude Code agents to interact with external services without custom API code.

content.title: MCP Server Catalog
content.summary: Reference catalog of 40+ popular MCP servers for Claude Code, organized by service category (marketing, analytics, project management, development, design, etc.). Includes setup commands, authentication requirements, and best practices for agent integration.

why.p: MCP servers provide standardized, type-safe interfaces to external services. Instead of writing custom API wrappers or shell scripts, agents can use a uniform tool interface to interact with GitHub, Slack, Notion, Stripe, and dozens of other platforms. This saves implementation time and reduces surface area for bugs.

The catalog organizes servers by category (Marketing & Ads, Product Analytics, Project Management, Development, Design, etc.), making it easy to discover which servers are available and find the right one for your task.

list.item: Agent needs to interact with an external service (GitHub, Slack, Notion, Stripe) without writing custom API code.
Standardizing tool interfaces across multiple agents — all agents use the same MCP tool, not ad-hoc curl calls.
Replacing fragile shell-script API wrappers with a typed, introspectable MCP interface.
Enabling Claude Code to read from or write to databases, CRMs, or project management tools mid-session.
The service has no MCP server and the task is one-off — use WebFetch + Bash(curl) directly instead.
The MCP server requires an OAuth flow that cannot be completed non-interactively — authentication will block.
Security policy disallows external process spawning or network calls from within Claude Code.
The catalog server's tool count is very large (>50 tools) — it pollutes the model's tool namespace; filter or build a focused custom server instead.

section.name: Marketing & Ads
section.p: Services for campaign management and advertising:

example.title: Google Ads setup
example.code: claude mcp add google-ads -s user -e GOOGLE_ADS_DEVELOPER_TOKEN=... -- npx -y google-ads-mcp

example.title: Meta Ads integration
example.code: claude mcp add meta-ads -s user -e META_ACCESS_TOKEN=... -- npx -y @pipeboard-co/meta-ads-mcp

rule.statement: Store API credentials in environment variables or 1Password; never hardcode tokens in settings.json.
rule.rationale: 1Password integration allows secure injection without exposing secrets in plaintext configuration files.

section.name: Product Analytics
section.p: Event tracking, funnels, and user cohort analysis:

example.title: PostHog analytics
example.code: claude mcp add posthog -s user -e POSTHOG_API_KEY=... -e POSTHOG_HOST=... -- npx -y posthog-mcp

example.title: Stripe payments
example.code: claude mcp add stripe -s user -e STRIPE_SECRET_KEY=... -- npx -y @stripe/mcp --tools=all

section.name: Project Management
section.p: Issue tracking, boards, sprints, and task management:

example.title: Jira setup
example.code: claude mcp add jira -s user \ -e JIRA_URL=https://your.atlassian.net \ -e JIRA_EMAIL=email \ -e JIRA_API_TOKEN=token \ -- npx -y @anthropic/jira-mcp

example.title: Linear issue tracking
example.code: claude mcp add linear -s user -e LINEAR_API_KEY=... -- npx -y @anthropic/linear-mcp

example.title: ClickUp tasks
example.code: claude mcp add clickup -s user -e CLICKUP_API_KEY=... -- npx -y @taazkareem/clickup-mcp-server

section.name: Development
section.p: GitHub, GitLab, databases, and infrastructure tools:

example.title: GitHub integration
example.code: claude mcp add github -s user -e GITHUB_TOKEN=... -- npx -y @anthropic/github-mcp

example.title: PostgreSQL database access
example.code: claude mcp add postgres -s user -e DATABASE_URL=postgres://... -- npx -y @anthropic/postgres-mcp

rule.statement: For databases: always use connection strings with read-only user credentials or restricted permissions when accessed by untrusted agents.
rule.rationale: Prevents agents from running destructive queries on production databases.

section.name: Design
section.p: Figma design-to-code and layer inspection:

example.title: Figma remote MCP
example.code: claude mcp add figma -s user -- npx -y @anthropic/figma-mcp

section.name: Knowledge & Docs
section.p: Airtable, Notion, Google Sheets, Confluence access:

example.title: Notion database access
example.code: claude mcp add notion -s user -e NOTION_API_KEY=... -- npx -y @notionhq/notion-mcp-server

example.title: Airtable spreadsheets
example.code: claude mcp add airtable -s user -e AIRTABLE_API_KEY=... -- npx -y airtable-mcp

section.name: Social Media
section.p: Twitter/X, Instagram, LinkedIn, Telegram posting and analytics:

example.title: Twitter comprehensive integration
example.code: claude mcp add twitter -s user \ -e TWITTER_API_KEY=... \ -e TWITTER_API_SECRET=... \ -e TWITTER_ACCESS_TOKEN=... \ -e TWITTER_ACCESS_SECRET=... \ -- npx -y mcp-twitter-server

example.title: Telegram messaging
example.code: claude mcp add telegram -s user \ -e TELEGRAM_API_ID=... \ -e TELEGRAM_API_HASH=... \ -- npx -y telegram-mcp

section.name: E-commerce
section.p: Stripe payments and Shopify product management:

example.title: Shopify store integration
example.code: claude mcp add shopify -s user \ -e SHOPIFY_STORE_URL=... \ -e SHOPIFY_ACCESS_TOKEN=... \ -- npx -y shopify-mcp

section.name: AI & Generative
section.p: OpenAI/DALL-E, Replicate models, ElevenLabs voice:

example.title: DALL-E image generation
example.code: claude mcp add dalle -s user -e OPENAI_API_KEY=... -- npx -y openai-mcp

example.title: ElevenLabs text-to-speech
example.code: claude mcp add elevenlabs -s user -e ELEVENLABS_API_KEY=... -- npx -y elevenlabs-mcp

section.name: Infrastructure
section.p: Cloudflare, Hetzner, AWS, Vercel deployments:

example.title: Cloudflare DNS and Workers
example.code: claude mcp add cloudflare -s user \ -e CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN=... \ -e CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID=... \ -- npx -y @cloudflare/mcp-server-cloudflare

example.title: Hetzner server management
example.code: claude mcp add hetzner -s user -e HETZNER_API_TOKEN=... -- npx -y mcp-hetzner

section.name: Browser & Automation
section.p: Puppeteer and Playwright for browser automation:

example.title: Playwright cross-browser testing
example.code: claude mcp add playwright -s user -- npx -y @anthropic/playwright-mcp

example.title: Puppeteer browser automation
example.code: claude mcp add puppeteer -s user -- npx -y @anthropic/puppeteer-mcp

section.name: Communication
section.p: Slack and Discord messaging:

example.title: Slack integration
example.code: claude mcp add slack -s user -e SLACK_BOT_TOKEN=xoxb-... -- npx -y @anthropic/slack-mcp

section.name: Memory
section.p: Persistent agent memory across sessions:

example.title: Memory MCP setup
example.code: claude mcp add memory -s user -- npx -y @anthropic/memory-mcp

antipattern.description: Using `npx -y` without pinning a version. This pulls the latest version on each Claude Code startup, causing silent breaking changes in upstream packages.
antipattern.reason: Version unpinning introduces non-deterministic behavior. Always specify a version: `npx -y @anthropic/github-mcp@1.2.3`.

antipattern.description: MCP server crashes are not surfaced clearly to the agent — they produce "tool unavailable" errors instead of actionable error messages.
antipattern.reason: Always verify server availability with `claude mcp list` before expecting agents to use a server.

antipattern.description: Storing tokens in plaintext in settings.json. `claude mcp add` writes credentials to `~/.claude/settings.json` without encryption.
antipattern.reason: Use 1Password integration or a secrets manager; inject tokens via environment variables, never hardcode.

antipattern.description: Scope servers to `user` scope for personal auth. Project scope only for team-shared, non-secret config.
antipattern.reason: Project-scoped secrets would be committed to version control.

antipattern.description: Agents cannot dynamically add MCP servers mid-session — all servers must be registered before Claude Code starts.
antipattern.reason: Use a setup agent or script (`mcp-setup.sh`) that runs once to register all required servers.

section.name: Agentic Workflow
section.p: Use a setup agent to check available servers, detect missing ones, and add them via `claude mcp add`. A specialized task agent then uses the configured MCP tools directly (tool names appear as `mcp__servername__toolname`). Keep server configuration in a documented `mcp-setup.sh` script committed to the project so it's reproducible across developers.

example.title: Idempotent MCP setup script
example.code:
#!/usr/bin/env bash # mcp-setup.sh — idempotent MCP server provisioning # Run once per developer machine. Requires env vars set.
set -euo pipefail
setup_mcp() { local name=$1; shift if claude mcp list 2>/dev/null | grep -q "^$name"; then echo "MCP $name already registered, skipping." return fi claude mcp add "$name" "$@" echo "MCP $name registered." }
setup_mcp github -s user -e GITHUB_TOKEN="$GITHUB_TOKEN" \ -- npx -y @anthropic/github-mcp
setup_mcp postgres -s user -e DATABASE_URL="$DATABASE_URL" \ -- npx -y @anthropic/postgres-mcp
setup_mcp memory -s user -- npx -y @anthropic/memory-mcp
echo "All MCP servers provisioned."

rule.statement: Test each server with `claude mcp list` before enabling agents to use it.
rule.rationale: If a server fails to start, the agent receives no error — it simply cannot see those tools. Verification prevents confusing "tool not found" failures.

rule.statement: For high-volume agent workflows, prefer direct API calls over MCP to avoid per-call process overhead.
rule.rationale: MCP servers run as subprocesses; each tool call spawns a new process. For latency-sensitive workloads, use direct HTTP calls instead.

section.name: Services Reference Matrix
section.p: Complete agent-friendliness matrix for popular MCP servers. Each entry includes authentication type, primary tools, and suitability for agent automation:

list.item: GitHub (`@anthropic/github-mcp`) - SaaS - Agent-friendly - Repos, PRs, issues; token auth
Notion (`@notionhq/notion-mcp-server`) - SaaS - Agent-friendly - Pages, databases; API key auth
Stripe (`@stripe/mcp`) - SaaS - Agent-friendly - Payments, subscriptions; API key
PostgreSQL (`@anthropic/postgres-mcp`) - OSS - Agent-friendly - Direct DB queries; connection string
Cloudflare (`@cloudflare/mcp-server-cloudflare`) - SaaS - Agent-friendly - DNS, Workers, KV, R2; API token
Slack (`@anthropic/slack-mcp`) - SaaS - Agent-friendly - Messages, channels; bot token
Linear (`@anthropic/linear-mcp`) - SaaS - Agent-friendly - Issues, cycles; API key
Figma (`@anthropic/figma-mcp`) - SaaS - Partial - Design-to-code; OAuth required
Memory (`@anthropic/memory-mcp`) - OSS - Agent-friendly - Persistent agent memory; local file
ElevenLabs (`elevenlabs-mcp`) - SaaS - Agent-friendly - TTS; API key
Hetzner (`mcp-hetzner`) - SaaS - Agent-friendly - Server management; API token
Playwright (`@anthropic/playwright-mcp`) - OSS - Agent-friendly - Browser automation; no auth

section.note: Agent-friendly means the server uses non-interactive authentication (API keys, tokens) that can be injected into settings.json. OAuth servers like Figma require manual authorization and may block automated agent workflows.

section.name: Checking Server Status
section.p: Verify server availability and configuration before using them in agent workflows:

example.title: List all installed MCP servers
example.code: claude mcp list

example.title: Interactive server testing with Inspector
example.code: npx @modelcontextprotocol/inspector -- npx @anthropic/github-mcp

rule.statement: Use the MCP Inspector tool to test a server's tools and resources interactively before deploying agents.
rule.rationale: Catch configuration errors, permission issues, and missing credentials in a sandbox before production use.

rule.statement: Always run `claude mcp list` before expecting agents to use a server — it confirms the server is installed and ready.
rule.rationale: Server startup failures are silent; agents simply cannot see the tools. Verification prevents false "tool not found" failures.

section.name: Recommended Subagents
section.p: Common agent patterns for MCP server workflows:

list.item: General-purpose Bash agent — runs `claude mcp add` commands and validates with `claude mcp list`
`faion-pm-agent` — uses GitHub/Linear/Jira MCP tools for issue management and tracking
`faion-research-agent` — uses memory MCP for persistent knowledge across multiple sessions
Database migration agent — uses PostgreSQL MCP to query and transform schema

rule.statement: Setup agents should run idempotently — checking for existing servers before adding new ones.
rule.rationale: Allows safe re-runs without duplicate registration errors or permission conflicts.

section.name: Agent Prompt Pattern
section.p: Correct format for instructing agents to use MCP tools:

example.title: Agent prompt using MCP tool
example.code: Use the mcp__github__create_issue tool to file an issue titled "{title}" in repo "{repo}". Set labels: ["bug"]. Body: {body}

example.title: Checking tool availability in agent
example.code: Before using MCP tools, verify with: 1. Run `claude mcp list` to check if the server is installed 2. Confirm the tool name in format `mcp__servername__toolname` 3. Check that required environment variables are set

rule.statement: MCP tool names use the format `mcp__servername__toolname`. The agent must know the exact tool name.
rule.rationale: Tool names don't have autodiscovery in agents. Test with Inspector first to find correct tool names.

section.name: CLI Tools Reference
section.p: Command-line tools for MCP server configuration and troubleshooting:

list.item: `claude mcp add` — Register an MCP server in user or project scope. Syntax: `claude mcp add {name} -s {scope} -e {ENV_VAR}=value -- {command}`
`claude mcp list` — List all registered servers and their status. Useful for verifying server availability before running agents
`claude mcp remove` — Unregister an MCP server. Use to clean up unused servers and reduce startup latency
`npx @modelcontextprotocol/inspector` — Interactive tool testing for a server. Allows exploration of available tools and resources before using in agents
`fastmcp dev` — Development mode for Python MCP servers with live reload (requires `pip install fastmcp`). Useful when developing custom MCP servers

rule.statement: Pin package versions when adding servers. Use `npx -y @anthropic/github-mcp@1.2.3` instead of `@latest`.
rule.rationale: Version pinning prevents silent breaking changes in upstream packages that could break agent workflows without warning.

rule.statement: Store API keys in 1Password or a secrets manager; inject via `op run` rather than hardcoding in settings.json.
rule.rationale: Keeps credentials out of plaintext config files and version control. 1Password CLI can inject secrets during setup.

rule.statement: Scope servers to user (`-s user`) for personal auth; use project scope (`-s project`) only for team-shared, non-secret config.
rule.rationale: User-scoped servers are personal and won't be committed. Project-scoped servers get saved in .git and could leak secrets.

rule.statement: Audit enabled MCP tools periodically — unused servers add startup latency and expand attack surface.
rule.rationale: Every additional server increases Claude Code startup time; disable unused servers to keep the tool namespace clean.

section.name: Agent-Friendly Authentication Patterns
section.p: Servers with non-interactive, token-based auth are compatible with agent automation:

list.item: API key / secret token (GitHub, Stripe, Linear) — Inject via environment variable. Non-interactive. Agent-friendly.
Bearer token (Slack, Notion, Cloudflare) — Inject via environment variable. Non-interactive. Agent-friendly.
OAuth with token refresh (Figma, Mixpanel hosted) — Requires manual authorization. Blocks automated agents.
Connection string (PostgreSQL, Redis) — Inject via environment variable. Non-interactive. Agent-friendly.
SSH key (Hetzner) — Can be injected via environment variable. Non-interactive if key is passwordless. Mostly agent-friendly.

section.note: OAuth servers like Figma require manual browser authorization, making them unsuitable for unattended agent workflows. Consider service accounts or token-based alternatives when possible.
